data.title: 5 Top Stocks For AI Fatigue
data.description:
AI Bubble Fears On The Rise

Fears of an AI bubble fueled volatility in recent weeks as big tech valuations stretched and Wall Street’s top executives warned of a market correction. The S&P 500’s AI-led rally sent prices to record highs last month, but a global tech sell-off has the index on track for its weakest November since 2008. The S&P 500 clawed back some losses in recent sessions, but several underlying indicators suggest market sentiment remains cautious.

Global AI-related investments are projected to exceed $4 trillion by 2030, reflecting investor zeal to capitalize on a technology that surged in popularity after ChatGPT was launched three years ago. Record spending by big tech persists despite skepticism about a near-term payoff as many organizations report zero measurable return from billions in capital investment. Tech sector valuations have soared to dot-com era levels - and, based on price-to-sales ratios, well beyond them. Some of AI’s biggest proponents acknowledge the fact that valuations are overinflated, including OpenAI chairman Bret Taylor:

AI will transform the economy… and create huge amounts of economic value in the future,” Taylor told The Verge. “I think we’re also in a bubble, and a lot of people will lose a lot of money.”

Alphabet’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) Sundar Pichai sees “elements of irrationality” in the current scale of AI investing, not unlike the excessive investment during the dot-com boom. If the AI bubble bursts, Pichai added, no company will be immune, including Alphabet, despite its breakthrough technology, Gemini, fueling gains.

In addition to stretched valuations, the way big tech is leaning heavily on circular deals and heavy debt to finance AI spending has raised eyebrows on Wall Street. The top five hyperscalers have raised a record $108B worth of debt in 2025 - more than 3x the average over the past nine years, according to data compiled by Bloomberg Intelligence. Moreover, some firms are turning to off-balance-sheet entities, including special-purpose vehicles (SPVs) - structures with a checkered history going back to Enron, D.A. Davidson analyst Gil Luria observed. A slowdown in AI demand could render the debt tied to these SPVs “worthless,” Luria warned, potentially triggering another financial crisis.

Beyond valuations, AI’s rapid expansion has masked some underlying economic issues. As US investment in AI-related industries surged over the past five years, non-AI investment remained stagnant, a Deutsche Bank research report revealed. And, outside of data centers, economic demand has been weak. In fact, the US would be close to a recession this year without technology-related spending, the report added. Meanwhile, a front-loading of chip orders has helped offset some of the tariff-driven constraints on global trade.

Q3 results, however, may signal a shift, with strength spilling over into areas outside the AI theme. The muted reaction to earnings beats — and sharp selloffs after misses — may indicate that investors are growing weary of AI-driven narratives, according to BlackRock analysts:

We feel this may suggest signs of investor AI fatigue. Concerns surrounding risks in private credit, a hawkish Fed pivot, and AI bubbles all emerged over the course of earnings season, likely spurring a bout of profit taking in select companies.”

Investors seeking to minimize AI bubble risk can diversify by looking at stocks outside the tech sector that also have strong investment fundamentals.

Top Stocks For AI Fatigue

SA Quant identified five Strong Buy-rated stocks with lower exposure to AI bubble risks, spanning the energy, materials, real estate, healthcare, and industrial sectors. Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings offer a data-driven evaluation of each stock by comparing more than 100 metrics to sector peers and assigning grades across five factors: Valuation, Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and EPS Revisions.

1. Par Pacific Holdings, Inc. (PARR)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.25B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing Stocks

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 2 out of 216

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 1 out of 18

The No. 2 quant-rated energy stock, Par Pacific Holdings operates a vertically integrated refining, logistics, and retail platform that supplies conventional and renewable fuels across niche markets, including Hawaii, the Rockies, and the Pacific Northwest. A member of the PRO Quant Portfolio, PARR has a system-wide refining capacity of 219K bpd, an integrated logistics network with 13 MMbbls of storage, and marine, rail, and pipeline assets, in addition to 119 fuel retail locations. The stock has crushed the market and energy sector in the past year, driving an A+ Momentum Grade, and has topped earnings expectations for three straight quarters.

PARR 1Y Price Return vs. S&P 500 Energy Sector (XLE)

The company has a 10-year track record of successful acquisitions aimed at increasing its refining scale and market reach. In recent years, PARR has also benefited from its investment in renewable fuels and joint ventures, adding new cash flow streams and hedging against energy transition and regulatory risk.​ PARR reported exceptional Q3 2025 results, with adjusted EPS outpacing estimates by $3.99 and revenue of $2.01B exceeding projections by $250M. The company achieved near-record system-wide throughput of 198K bpd in the quarter. Q3 results were bolstered by a small refinery exemption gain of $202.6M, while $100M in proceeds were achieved from the closing of the Hawaii Renewables joint venture with Mitsubishi and ENEOS.

Outstanding growth in EBITDA and operating cash flow in the past year underpin an A+ Growth Grade. Although EBITDA margins have dragged on its factor grade, PARR showcases excellent return on equity and total capital. Despite soaring momentum, the stock is trading at an attractive valuation with a P/E of 7x, representing a 47% discount to the sector. PARR is a niche energy player with a proven track record of profitable growth, excellent momentum, and my top Strong Buy pick for hedging AI bubble risk.

2. Guardant Health, Inc. (GH)

  • Market Capitalization: $13.95B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Health Care

  • Industry: Health Care Services

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 5 out of 936

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 1 out of 48

No. 1 among quant-rated Healthcare Services stocks, Guardant Health is a precision oncology company that develops blood-based and tissue-based genomic tests to detect, monitor, and guide treatment for cancer. Guardant has shown excellent momentum in the past year, surging after beating Q3 earnings and boosting its full-year outlook.

Guardant 1Y Price Return vs. S&P 500 Health Care Sector (XLV)

Guardant's top line rose nearly 39% YoY in Q3 to $265M as the company surpassed $1B in annualized revenue for the first time. Guardant saw strong performance across all business segments: Oncology, bipharma, and screening, led by stellar volume growth in the Guardant360 Liquid, Guardant360 Tissue, and Reveal product lines. Guardant solid gross margins expanded further in the prior-year period. This improvement was primarily driven by a significant reduction in Reveal COGS, which fell from $1,000 per test to $500 per test.

Over the past year, Guardant has delivered robust growth in revenue, which is expected to rise from $739M in FY 2024 to $1.97B by FY 2028, according to consensus estimates. Guardant has positive momentum in Wall Street analyst estimates as reflected by 15 upward earnings revisions in the past three months and just one down revision. A high-momentum healthcare stock with high long-term growth prospects is a great addition for balancing a portfolio.

3. Omega Healthcare Investors, Inc. (OHI)

  • Market Capitalization: $14.04B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Health Care REITs

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 3 out of 174

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 1 out of 17

Omega Healthcare Investors is a high-yielding, top quant-ranked healthcare REIT with more than 1,000 properties in the United States and UK and $11.4B in total real estate investments, focused on skilled nursing and assisted living. OHI invests in long-term healthcare facilities to generate profitable shareholder returns by offering triple-net leases and fixed-rate mortgage loans to a diverse group of healthcare operators.

OHI 1Y Price Return vs. S&P 500 Real Estate Sector (XLRE)

In Q3 2025, OHI beat earnings estimates for the eighth straight quarter with adjusted FFO per share of $0.78, beating by $0.02, and revenue of $311.59M (+12.9% YoY), beating by $50M. Results were driven by an increase in facility acquisitions, lease extensions, and other rent escalations. OHI increased its full-year guidance, supported by improving occupancy and coverage, CEO Taylor Pickett explained in a release:

We again delivered strong quarterly results, as we continue to grow FAD per share while further de-levering the balance sheet,” Pickett said. "The backdrop continues to be favorable. Operating metrics remain strong, with both occupancy and coverage further improving in the quarter. The pipeline is very active, and we have a strong balance sheet and cost of capital that should allow us to continue to accretively invest."

OHI’s AFFO/revenue margin of over 80% underpins its ‘A’ Profitability Grade, along with robust cash flow and return on capital. OHI EPS is projected to grow by 7% in FY25 and revenue by 22% to $1.07B. OHI’s dividend has an excellent yield and is expected to continue growing in the next three years, according to consensus estimates. OHI offers a dividend yielding 5.85%, for a B+ Dividend Yield Grade, with 21 consecutive years of payments. A high payout ratio weighs on OHI’s dividend safety score, although the REIT maintains solid interest coverage and conservative leverage.

OHI is trading in line with the sector at a P/AFFO of 15x - but a solid dividend, robust AFFO margins, and high momentum make it a Strong Buy REIT.

4. Ardagh Metal Packaging S.A. (AMBP)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.24B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials

  • Industry: Metal, Glass & Plastic Container Stocks

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 34 out of 267

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 2 out of 10

A global can manufacturer with leading market positions in Europe, North America, and Brazil, Ardagh has steadily grown revenue and EBITDA over the past three years, benefiting from secular tailwinds, as aluminum cans continue to gain share of the global beverage packaging mix. AMBP has held a Strong Buy or Buy quant rating since the end of April, driven by consistently solid profitability, EPS revision, and valuation factor grades.

Ardagh 1Y Price Return vs. S&P 500 Materials Sector (XLB)

Q3 revenue rose 8% to $1.43B and EBITDA +6% to $208M, driven by shipment growth in Europe and North America, lower operational costs, and a favorable category mix. Global volumes were up 2%, driven by growth in energy drinks, ciders, ready-to-drink teas and coffees, wines and water - offsetting weakness in the beer category. Ardagh expects low single-digit market growth in 2026, although it projects potential headwinds from the tariff situation in the next 12 to 18 months. Revenue growth has been solid in the past year, although the company’s growth grade has been dragged down by an inconsistent dividend. Ardagh is expected to continue growing net income and revenue over the next three years, and has six upward earnings revisions and one downward revision.

Trading at 5.9x cash flow, a 37% discount from the sector, is supporting an A+ Valuation Grade. Ardagh offers a dividend yielding 10.67%, although it suffers from weak safety, growth, and consistency. Ardagh has solid growth prospects and is riding green metal structural tailwinds, with low exposure to AI trends.

5. Global Ship Lease, Inc. (GSL)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.25B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Marine Transportation

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 4 out of 596

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 1 out of 24

The No. 1 quant-rated Marine Transportation stock and recent addition to the PRO Quant Portfolio, GSL is a high-yielding, highly profitable small-cap security that has crushed the market in the past year and is trading at a significant discount to the sector. Headquartered in Athens, Greece, GSL owns and charters mid-sized and smaller containerships to major global shipping companies.

GSL 1Y Price Return vs. S&P 500 Industrial Sector (XLI)

GSL delivered solid financial results in Q3 2025 with revenue of $192.7M (+10% YoY) and normalized EPS of $2.62 (+8.3% YoY), marking an eighth straight earnings beat. GSL’s $1.92B contract backlog helps shield against market volatility and includes 100% forward contract cover for 2025, 96% for 2026, and 74% for 2027. Despite the cyclicality of marine transportation, Global Ship Lease is capitalizing on headwinds and trade policy uncertainty - including new emissions rules and last-minute deferral of US and Chinese port surcharges, as highlighted by Executive Chairman George Youroukos in the latest earnings call:

All of these real and potential factors are contributing to two major effects, both of which play to our advantage. Number one, making supply chains less efficient, which means that more ships are needed to transport a given quantity of cargo; and number two, increasing the value of flexible, midsized and smaller container ships such as those in our fleet.”

GSL’s A+ Profitability Grade is underpinned by exceptional operating margins, return on equity and total capital, along with impressive capex/sales. GSL offers a stable and growing dividend that's yielding 7.13%, and showcasing a safe payout ratio of 20.26%. GSL’s dividend has a 3Y CAGR of 15.6%, supporting an ‘A’ Dividend Growth Grade. GSL reinstituted dividend payments in 2021 after suspending them in 2016 due to the severe downturn in the container shipping industry.

GSL is trading at a mere 3.4x earnings, an 83% discount to the sector, for an A+ Valuation Grade. Although the Marine Transportation industry can be volatile amid rapidly changing geopolitical and global factors, GSL is well positioned to navigate complexities while maximizing strategic optionality in both fleet deployment and balance sheet management. In addition, bullish momentum, strong profitability, and an excellent valuation make GSL a great pick to wind up my list of top stocks for investors looking for protection if the AI bubble bursts.

Best Stocks for Hedging AI Fatigue

AI bubble fears have fueled market volatility as tech capital spending hits record levels, valuations get stretched, and the much-hyped technologies fail to deliver returns for organizations. AI investments could reach $4 trillion by 2030, just as echoes of the dot-com era have Wall Street leaders warning of a market correction. Even AI proponents acknowledge the risk of the AI bubble bursting - a development that would spare no one, even big tech giants. Investors could diversify portfolios in several ways, including by looking at stocks in sectors less likely to get hit hard by an AI implosion. data.title: SLB: Meeting The Red Queen Moment
data.description:
Introduction

As I read through the Schlumberger, (SLB) call one thing became abundantly clear. This company bears little resemblance to the company I left 10 years ago. One metric to judge the true depth of this change is profit per employee. In 2015 with 95K employees the company made a profit of $21,810 per. Last year with 110,000 employees, SLB made a profit of $40,550 per. Something is going right.

The last decade has forced a lot of changes on the oilfield service-OFS industry, including notionally, changing the definition of what constitutes oilfield service. The old paradigm of capital and labor intensive district-style operations with big pieces of equipment, ready to mobilize to the field with dozens of field techs was dying.

Starting in 2015, the industry shrank so fast that millions of dollars of fancy kit sat bleaching in the sun, with the field techs long since laid off. SLB wrote off the value of billions of dollars in acquisitions in the late teens, they'd taken prior to 2015. This included the $10 bn they paid for Smith International, and most of the $15 bn they paid for Cameron-well heads, in the 2010 era. Big dreams had gone bust. The company and investors paid for it.

The Red Queen was swinging her metaphoric axe, and companies that could not adapt, died out. But SLB met the moment and changed. Technology-eventually AI, and subscription services rose to take the place of big, heavy equipment model. Now much more of their revenue and profits are starting to come from Digital sales and subscription sources, that have a much smaller incremental capex footprint than the old model.

In fact it's grown to a point where Digital is now a separate reporting division, comprised of Platform & Applications, Digital Operations, Digital Exploration and Professional Services. To be clear, Digital is now performing at a run-rate of $2.4 bn annually with recurring-meaning subscription, revenue approaching the $1 bn mark-forecast for 2026, and up 7% YoY. This is high margin business with margins logging in at 32.7% in Q-3-forecast to 35% in Q-4. Olivier Le Peuch, CEO of SLB discussed the "whole" of company approach to Digital, and how it will outperform Core OFS-

Digital growth. And hence, this one will outperform the Core because the principle we are setting here is essentially for every service we provide, for every well site we touch, for every equipment we deliver, we'll progressively add building on our platform and connecting to our Live Performance Center, will add a set of Digital services and enhance this offering that enhance the operation, the performance and get differentiation and get the customer to create more value.

This is transformational, and the impact for the company cannot be overstated. I last covered SLB with a Hold in Aug, 2023.

The thesis for SLB

While OFS remains the "Core" of the company's service matrix, it's shrinking. It's not even really appropriate to call SLB an OFS company any longer, although they remain the Big Blue daddy in that space. SLB at current levels discounts any growth in their core service lines, the contributions the ChampionX acquisition has been making, and doesn't reflect at all the growth in digital and subscription revenue.

SLB noted an improvement in fundamentals with activity levels stabilizing, and cited the need globally to overcome production declines with new drilling and exploration. It was also noted that in international markets, long term capacity expansion plans to ensure energy security will spur new drilling. OPEC+, mainly KSA and Russia, are also running out of excess capacity in legacy fields, which should spur new drilling in 2026. The project pipeline in deepwater markets appears to be targeted to a large number of FID's in 2026-7. SLB was confident that international markets led by offshore and NOC/IOC activity in Asian countries, underpinned by demand for gas will create a rebound in their Core services.

Against that backdrop SLB seems like a good bet at current levels. The Wall Street guru's are positive on the company with a Buy rating, and price targets that range up to $62.00 over the next year. The median of the analyst's estimates is $43.00, which is probably more realistic, but less enticing from its current price.

Over the past year, SLB has underperformed the big cap OFS cohort, despite beating EPS estimate 3 out of 4 quarters. A lot of that we can attribute to adverse commodity prices restraining activity during the time period.

Catalysts for SLB

I learned something in the call. SLB is going after Data Center construction business. This was noted in the call by analyst, Scott Gruber of Citigroup-

Gruber- I want to ask about the data solutions business -- so the data center solutions business, it's growing pretty quickly here. It's actually becoming fairly sizable. Can you talk about the strategy for the business? Is the aim here to develop a skill set and take it global as data center construction goes global? And overall, how do we think about the growth for the data center solutions business in '26 and beyond?

Le Peuch- Yes. I think it's early days, and we're very pleased with the market position we gained in a very fast pace. I think based on our first relationship and partnership with the hyperscalers gave us the opportunity to step in into that market, building on our manufacturing engineering process technology and global supply and logistics that I think we have put to make it a reality. Now going forward, yes, the ambition is to expand beyond the U.S. footprint we have established, and we already have a pipeline of expansion here in Asia that has been agreed and to also expand to more customers and diversify hyperscalers and colocators as we call them, to complement our offering.

But yes, we will add technology. We will add the critical technology that make it unique to go beyond the first step we had. So yes, we have an ambition to grow it, to expand customers, to expand geography, to broaden our offering. And remember, this is clearly not driven by oil and gas customers. It's driven by our hyperscalers partners that reach out to us to help them respond to this AI boom and data center growth that I think will last beyond this decade, clearly.

Here is a graphic showing growth estimates for this market to 2032.

At first thought this doesn't seem like a natural fit for the company, but then you look at how they've organized the business and it's clear that it calls on all of their key skill sets. I would put SLB up against any of the companies mentioned in this article as being leader's in the Data Center field.

I don't want to oversell this new business line. As Le Peuch said, "It's early days." We will have to see how it develops, but the TAM-total addressable market is enormous and growing at an 11.7 CAGR. If SLB got even a 0.001% of this market it would equate to huge boost in revenues in 2032. That seems doable as they reported $331 mm for Data Center Solutions in the quarter, a boost of 140% for the period a year ago. I'd say they are off to a good start in this segment. We will leave that there.

We should also note the closing of the ChampionX merger with the accretive cash flow of $579 mm in revenue and $108 mm in pretax income reported for Q-3. SLB also announced two additional acquisitions to further round out its Digital portfolio- Stimline Digital and RESMAN Energy Technologies. Stimline focuses on AI powered well intervention and optimization. RESMAN brings tracer and monitoring technologies. These are useful in determining areas of unwanted flow or loss within the reservoir.

Q-3 and guidance

Overall, SLB third quarter revenue of $8.9 billion increased $382 million or 4% sequentially. Company-wide adjusted EBITDA margin for the third quarter was 23.1%, representing a sequential decrease of 92 basis points. Segment by segment results were as follows-

Digital- Third quarter Digital revenue of $658 million increased 11% sequentially, and adjusted EBITDA was $215 million, reflecting a margin of 32.7%, up 123 basis points sequentially.

Reservoir Performance- Revenue of $1.7 billion declined 1% sequentially as higher activity in Europe and Africa was more than offset by lower revenue in the Middle East and Asia, primarily in Saudi Arabia.

Well Construction- Revenue of $3 billion was flat sequentially as higher revenue in offshore Guyana and North America were offset by lower drilling activity in Saudi Arabia and Argentina.

Production Systems- Reported revenue of $3.5 billion increased $542 million or 18% sequentially. This reflects 2 months of activity from the acquired ChampionX production chemicals and artificial lift businesses, which contributed $575 million of revenue. SLB expects these results will be further enhanced by the $400 million of annual pretax synergies that they expect to generate within the first 3 years.

During the quarter, SLB generated $1.7 billion of cash flow from operations and $1.1 billion of free cash flow. Full year, Capex, including the impact of ChampionX should be approximately $2.4 billion.

SLB repurchased $114 million of stock during the quarter, which brings their total stock repurchases to $2.4 billion on a year-to-date basis. When combined with the $1.6 billion dividend commitment for the year, the company is returning a total of $4 billion to shareholders for the full year

Guidance

SLB expects the fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA margin to expand 50 to 150 bps sequentially. This will be driven primarily by increased earnings contribution from both Digital and Production Systems end-of-year sales, including a full quarter of ChampionX results and fully restored operations on our APS Ecuador assets. Specific to the Digital business, they expect a significant increase in the fourth quarter on seasonally higher sales across the portfolio. As a result, SLB believes the Digital Division will be able to achieve double-digit growth year-on-year with EBITDA margin reaching 35% on a full year basis.

Company filings

Risks

The company still faces headwinds in its core oil and gas businesses. The company's upbeat view of the logjam of FID's breaking loose could be further delayed. Nothing is certain until it happens. If we don't see increasing revenues and margins the stock price will probably languish in the same range it's traded in since early April-sub $40.00. Absent a big breakdown in crude or gas, I don't see a lot of downside from here, although there could be some as I will discuss in the close.

Are we in an AI bubble? There is a frenzied feel to the exponential growth of this sector. Parallels have been drawn to the internet bust of 2000. Just as growth in AI data centers could be a catalyst for SLB, retrenchment in the event of a reset could put a drag on their shares.

Your takeaway

We certainly haven't seen a decisive move higher in crude yet. Just the reverse in recent trading. Winter is yet to come to deplete gas inventories. Also the Russian sanctions are too newly in place to properly handicap their true impact. The Russians wrote the book on thumbing their nose at the Western world. It's even odds they will do it this time, blunting the impact of sanctions.

That said I think SLB is near a cyclical low having touched and bounced off $32 a number of times since April. The new Digital business is an outlier that's off to a very promising start. That said, the tone from management was strongly bullish about the growth potential for Digital. CEO Le Peuch comments-

I believe that the scale of our offering, the capability and the opportunity we have, I don't see constraints into the market. So I believe that, okay, the growth potential we have from both the Digital operation today, if you compare the Digital operation, if you were to do the math, okay, we could say that this represents 1% of revenue of our Core, why not 50% of revenue of our Core in the future. So that's the way you could look into it, okay? We have 1,500 customers. Only a few portion of them have adopted our platform. We are in early innings of AI.

I think despite our obvious desire to add to or take a position, given the 13% rally in SLB share's proximity to the new tariff regime, my recommendation is to wait for a pullback toward that $32.00 level. Not being too strict about it though, we don't want to miss a big move higher for $0.50 cents. Long term, you want to own SLB. Growth will return to the upstream sector. It must.

The decline in Energy Return on Investment-EROI and the explosion globally of M2 in relation to oil out put will demand it. Shanaka Anslem Perera, in an article on his Substack entitled, "The Monetary Energy Singularity," writes-

As of September 2025, the United States M2 money supply stands at $22.21 trillion, representing a 40% increase from pre-pandemic levels. During this same period, global oil production remained essentially flat at approximately 100 million barrels per day. When we calculate the ratio of West Texas Intermediate crude oil price to M2 money supply, we arrive at 0.00259—a figure that matches, with eerie precision, the troughs of 1998, 2016, and 2020.

Each previous occurrence preceded supply disruptions and price explosions of 150% to 200% within 18 to 24 months.

Conventional oil fields discovered in the 1930s through 1960s delivered EROI ratios of 100:1. By the 1990s, this had declined to 30:1 for new conventional discoveries. Today’s figures reveal a system approaching criticality: deepwater offshore now averages 10:1, while marginal US shale formations have collapsed to 5:1.

I find his analysis interesting and compelling. If it is even half right, we are setup for a serious dislocation in energy prices, that will create intense demand for support services in the upstream energy sector. A situation that should be very positive for SLB.

data.title: Pure Storage Is Too Expensive For My Liking
data.description:
One very interesting company that has really taken off in recent months is Pure Storage, Inc. (PSTG). To be honest with you, the first time I ever heard about the business, I thought that it was some sort of self-storage company. But it certainly does not fit under that category. In fact, it operates as a leader in data management and storage services. In my last article about the business, published in late August of this year, I went into more detail about this. But in short, it uses all flash functionality to help customers increase cloud native applications and to monetize data infrastructure. A big part of its mission right now is to help customers accelerate their AI adoption.

As exciting as this space is, in my previous article about the business, I called it a Sell candidate. This might seem surprising given how rapid the growth in the AI market is. However, even though the business itself is doing exceptionally well, shares looked very expensive. They were so expensive, in fact, that I did not believe that any realistic growth would justify meaningful upside. Since then, however, the stock has outperformed the market, jumping 23% while the S&P 500 (SP500) is up 5.5%. This seems to indicate that, for now at least, Mr. Market disagrees with me. But at the end of the day, I suspect that shares will underperform for an extended window of time.

It is very possible that I am wrong. Even though I don't think I am, as new data comes in, it is necessary to revisit the picture and evaluate what kind of situation investors are faced with. It just so happens that, on December 2nd, management will be announcing financial results for the third quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, there is a lot of optimism from both management and analysts. But unless management comes out with something really exceptional, I don't think that my overall assessment here will have been wrong. Because of that, I think that maintaining it as a Sell candidate is appropriate.

Checking In On Pure Storage

Before I get into what analysts anticipate for third quarter earnings, I do think it would be useful to touch on, just briefly, overall financial performance for the business in recent memory. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2023 through 2025 fiscal years. And in the chart below, you can see results for the first half of 2026 compared to the same time of 2025. I already covered these details in my prior article. But in short, the company is benefiting from significant user growth as demand for its services, as well as new product offerings, come about. By the second quarter of 2026, the company serviced 62% of Fortune 500 customers, with the total customer count exceeding 13,500. Back in the second quarter of 2023, it worked with only 56% of Fortune 500 firms, and serviced around 10,500 customers.

Overall, this trend is great to see. And management has done a great job. The current expectation that analysts have is that this performance, from a fundamental standpoint, will continue. For the third quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, for instance, they expect revenue of $956.5 million. That would be up 15.1% over the $831.1 million the company reported a year earlier. This might sound extraordinary. But it's important to know that they aren't the only ones who expect this kind of year over year sales increase. Management mentioned, when they announced results for the second quarter of 2026, that revenue would come in at between $950 million and $960 million. So, analysts are right around the midpoint of that range.

There's also the expectation that bottom-line results will improve also. The current belief that analysts have is that earnings per share will come in at $0.24. That's up nicely from the $0.19 per share reported the same time last year and would translate to a rise in net profits from $63.6 million to $82.4 million. On an adjusted basis, earnings per share are expected to total around $0.58. That would represent an improvement over the $0.50 per share reported a year ago and would mean a rise in adjusted net income from $171.4 million to $199.2 million.

It is noteworthy that management has not provided any estimates when it comes to earnings per share. They have, however, provided other profitability guidance. They anticipate, for instance, non-GAAP operating income for the quarter of between $185 million and $195 million. At the midpoint, this would be 13.6% above the $167.3 million the company reported for last year. Considering that the adjusted earnings per share estimates call for a rise of 16% compared to the same time last year, it is unlikely that analysts are off by much, if at all. In the table above, you can also see what certain cash flow metrics were for the company in the third quarter of 2025. It is highly likely that if revenue, earnings, and adjusted earnings, all increase year over year, that these cash flow metrics will also.

One thing that, I believe, would be particularly interesting will be data that management offers when it comes to its different offerings. Examples of offerings would be those that fall under the "product" category and the "subscription services" category. The product category includes things like physical hardware systems and one time software licenses that are sold to customers. Examples here include its FlashArray systems, FlashBlade systems, and its Portworx Enterprises licenses. Meanwhile, its subscription services category involves regular software updates and maintenance services, fully managed storage subscription offerings, and a wide array of other service offerings that bring in recurring and predictable revenue.

By management's own admission, they are continuing to shift in the direction of prioritizing subscription services. The stability in revenue and cash flows certainly is a contributor here. But there are other reasons behind this as well. In the chart above, you can see the overall product and subscription services revenues, gross profits, and gross profit margins that the company has achieved over the last three completed fiscal years and for the first half of 2026 compared to the same time of 2025.

As you can tell, the product side of the business has become increasingly challenged in recent years, particularly when it comes to profits. At the same time, however, management has been pushing to achieve greater subscription revenue, and with that growth has come higher margins. Take results for the first half of 2026 as an example. The margin difference between products and subscription services has become massive. If all of the revenue the company generated during the first half of the 2026 fiscal year came from subscription services, then total gross profit generated by the company would be $168.9 million more than if all of the revenue came from product sales.

The deliberate effort that management has made to make this transition is very quickly turning the subscription services side of the business into the real value driver for investors. From 2023 through 2024, product sales dropped from 65.1% of total sales down to 53.6%. By the first half of the 2026 fiscal year, they had finally dipped to 49.9%. Subscription services revenue, meanwhile, has taken the lead. Furthermore, subscription services have gone from accounting for only 35.6% of total gross profit for the company back in 2023 to 49.2% last year. And for the first half of the 2026 fiscal year, this increased further to 53.8%.

Investors should continue to monitor the companies transition from a product oriented one to a subscription services one. But that's not the only thing that they should keep an eye out for. Management has come out with multiple news items over the last couple of months. In late October, for instance, they announced what is essentially a bundled, validated architecture for enterprise scale AI deployment that covers multiple areas like compute, storage, networking, and software. This has been done in coordination with Cisco (CSCO) and Nvidia (NVDA), with the latter party’s technology validating this development. The objective on this front is to make the barrier to AI adoption lower for enterprises, meaning that costs should reduce and the time to fully adopt AI infrastructure contract.

There have been other noteworthy developments as well. In late September, as an example, management made public that it had made certain enhancements to its Pure Storage Platform that should, in theory, make it easier for corporations to use AI to the utmost. This involved the introduction of Pure Storage Cloud, which the company has positioned as an "Azure Native" offering that empowers customers to store managed data in public cloud environments while also helping to provide automation, governance, data mobility, and data management, no matter where the data happens to be. That includes on premise, in the cloud, or even in a hybrid environment.

These types of offerings pave the way for even more AI adoption as time goes on. And normally, this is something that I would be excited about. But unfortunately, the downside is that shares are expensive and getting more expensive by the day.

In the chart above, you can see how the stock is valued using estimates for the 2026 fiscal year and historical results for 2025. That chart also shows how the company was valued when I wrote about it earlier this year. To be clear, when it comes to the EBITDA, a big differentiator in it compared to adjusted operating cash flow has to do with stock-based compensation. I don't add that back in because it is a real expense at the end of the day, even if it's non-cash in nature. This, I believe, is a more accurate depiction of how expensive shares are since management would theoretically have to pay out that stock-based compensation in the form of cash if paying out in stock was not possible or was intensely undesirable. The adjusted operating cash flow, by comparison, ignores this.

Takeaway

Operationally speaking, Pure Storage is a fascinating business that's doing a phenomenal job. It is in an interesting phase of its life, with a continued transition away from products and toward subscription services. Margins should expand as this progresses. And right now, analysts and management are both optimistic about the future. I, on the other hand, am concerned about the price that investors have to pay for this growth. To me, this is a bridge too far, and it certainly justifies the Sell rating I have on the company.

data.title: First Eagle Global Income Builder Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Shaking off the second quarter’s volatility, equity markets marched steadily higher throughout the third.

Though risk and uncertainty remained in ample supply during the summer, stocks continued to rebound off their April “Liberation Day” troughs as investors cheered continued artificial intelligence (AI) spending and the prospect of Federal Reserve easing. For the quarter, the S&P 500 Index gained 8.1%, led to new record highs by the market’s tech-oriented growth names. The MSCI EAFE Index returned 4.8% in the third quarter but maintains a year-to-date advantage of more than 1,000 basis points over US stocks. Likewise, US fixed income markets have also outperformed global issues in the third quarter, but have lagged year to date. The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index returned 0.6% and 7.9% for the third quarter and year to date, respectively, compared to 2.0% and 6.1% for the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index.1

Market Valuations Suggest Lofty Expectations, Particularly in the US

At the risk of sounding like a broken record, we’ll again point out that risk perception in the US markets appears quite low. Equity market valuation multiples are rich, high yield spreads are tight, implied volatility is low, and the price ratio of growth to value stocks is near its all-time high.2

In short, we think it’s fair to characterize the appetite for risk in the US as full—which is not to say market prices have no foundation in reality. Earnings growth forecasts remain constructive, driven by factors ranging from the impact of the AI capex cycle to fairly accommodative fiscal conditions. In terms of the latter, the rate of growth in government spending and the size of its debt continue to exceed wage growth, imparting some positive nominal drift to the economy that has trickled down into expectations for corporate earnings and margins expectations. At the same time, short-term interest rates have moved lower following September’s widely expected 25 basis point reduction in the federal funds rate, and markets are pricing in an additional 50 basis points of cuts by year-end.3

Similarly constructive dynamics have emerged in non-US economies, beckoning the return of animal spirits to markets sorely in need of a boost and helping to drive the relative outperformance on non-US equity markets year to date. In Europe, for example, Germany has taken steps to leverage its ample fiscal capacity, notably on defense and infrastructure projects,4 and NATO countries as a whole agreed to raise annual defense spending to 5% of GDP by 2035.5 China’s multiyear debt-restructuring initiative for local governments— earmarked at 12 trillion yuan—appears to be bearing fruit, enabling local authorities to clear arrears to suppliers rather than debt service.6 In Japan, the country’s likely next—and first female—prime minister has a reputation as a pro-spending conservative who also favors stimulative monetary policy.7

Considerable weakness in the dollar over the course of 2025—the ICE US Dollar Index is down nearly 10% year to date—has further supported the outperformance of non-US equity markets. Still, it wasn’t until this year that the MSCI EAFE Index broke through its 2007 peak.8 The MSCI EAFE is trading around 17x trailing earnings—not cheap but well within the realm of normal, from our perspective, and certainly less rich than the S&P 500’s 28x.9 And while it has pulled back marginally, the price ratio of US equities to non-US stocks stands at more than 2.5 times the average since 1970.10

Unusually, Gold and Equities Have Surged in Tandem

An interesting feature of the financial markets over the past several quarters has been the concurrent rally in equities and gold prices. Gold’s year-to-date gain of nearly 50% puts it on an annual pace not seen in nearly 50 years, as central banks and investors alike have piled into the metal amid elevated risks and the potential for currency debasement. More recently, we’ve also seen other precious metals—including silver and platinum—break out to the upside.11

Historically, equities and gold have both participated in the nominal drift of the global economy, but they typically have done so in a countercyclical manner, as gold has tended to thrive in conditions less supportive of equity investment. There have been exceptions, however, perhaps most notably during the early 1970s. This period was characterized by the fiscal pressures of the Vietnam War, the end of the Bretton Woods gold peg and executive branch pressure on the Fed to ease interest rates despite inflation pressures, a combination of factors that contributed to monetary disequilibrium and a decade of stagflation.

While we don’t want to overstate the historical analogies, there are some evident parallels between that period and the current environment, including the fact that the US appears to be a long way from home base in terms of monetary and fiscal settings. For example, we wouldn’t expect the federal government to be running at a substantial fiscal deficit over the past several years—including just shy of 6.0% of GDP in the recently completed fiscal year—with sub-4.5% unemployment levels.12 Meanwhile, Trump is pressuring the Fed to lower the federal funds rate while the fiscal imbalance remains unaddressed and geopolitical tail risks have only grown fatter.

Tending the Garden

Strong markets can create a dilemma for portfolio managers; while you want to let your roses bloom, you must remain true to your investment discipline. Viewing our portfolios from the bottom up, we have looked for opportunities to recycle capital, trimming more successful positions to take advantage of other opportunities in sectors or geographies that have been out of favor.

Of course, the world remains rife with risk, including massive sovereign debt loads, geopolitical frictions and political strife. While an increase in the market’s perception of these risks would likely impact both US and international stocks, international markets in general appear to be priced with less expectational risk compared to the US, given current valuations. Regardless, we expect our investment discipline to support our efforts to provide long-term real returns while avoiding the permanent impairment of capital.

Portfolio Review

Global Income Builder Fund A Shares (FEBAX)(without sales charge*) posted a return of 5.08% in the third quarter 2025. As of September 30, 2025, the Fund’s equity allocation was 63.86%, comprising 41.78% international stocks and 22.08% US stocks. The Fund’s 20.77% bond allocation included 15.17% in investment grade issues. The Global Income Builder Fund outperformed the composite index in the period.

Leading equity contributors in the First Eagle Global Income Builder Fund this quarter included gold bullion, Jardine Matheson Holdings Limited (OTCPK:JMHLY) and British American Tobacco plc (BTI). Fixed income contributors included Mexico Remittances, 12.5%, due 10/15/2031; Government of Brazil 10.0%, due 1/1/2027; and Government of the United States of America 1.25%, due 4/15/2028.

The gold price continued to set new record nominal highs during the quarter, propelled by a rare convergence of monetary policy uncertainty, attacks on Federal Reserve independence, disruptive trade policy, shifting fiscal policies, heightened local and geopolitical tensions, and high sovereign debt levels.

Shares of Hong Kong’s Jardine Matheson Holdings rebounded during the quarter along with a broader recovery for many Chinese-listed equities. More specific to Jardine, the company’s disciplined focus on capital allocation boosted the performance of its publicly traded subsidiaries. Even after these gains, the discount at the holding company level remains significant.

British American Tobacco (BAT) is the world’s second-largest tobacco company. Higher pricing offset lower volumes for US combustibles during the recent quarter, and BAT’s alternative products were strong. The company’s modern oral product Velo—with an already strong, established presence in Europe—won traction and gained share in the US. Additionally, the hold of BAT’s brand VUSE in the US vapor market could be strengthened as the Trump administration pressures the import of illegal vape products, primarily from China. Supported by its portfolio of strong brands, BAT has a history of returning cash to shareholders through dividends and buybacks.

The Mexico remittances bonds referenced above are secured by a first lien on the remittance cash flow from the US to Mexico through the money-transfer business of Nueva Elektra del Milenio, the retail subsidiary of Mexican conglomerate Grupo Elektra. Despite recent shifts in US immigration policy, a steeper yield curve bolstered these bonds during the quarter. Remittance volumes were buoyed by the favorable exchange rate on repatriations converted from the weaker US dollar into the stronger Mexican peso.

Brazil’s shorter-duration sovereign bonds were bolstered during the quarter by stronger local currency and demand for government debt. The Banco Central do Brasil paused interest rate hikes during July—retaining a still-restrictive 15% rate—which is attractive relative to other emerging and developed market debt on both a nominal and real basis.

Shorter-dated US government debt performed well as short-term interest rates moved lower following September’s widely expected 25 basis point reduction in the federal funds rate. Markets are pricing in an additional 50 basis points of cuts by year end.

Equity detractors in the quarter included Philip Morris International Inc. (PM), Shimano Inc. (OTCPK:SHMDF) and Fuchs SE (OTCPK:FUPBY) preferred registered shares. Nearly all fixed income positions contributed to performance during the quarter given favorable market conditions; the lowest contributions came from Nufarm Australia Ltd. 5.0%, due 1/27/2030; Mileage Plus Holdings LLC 6.5%, due 6/20/2027; and AMN Healthcare Inc. 6.5%, due 1/15/2031.

Tobacco company Philip Morris reported better-than-expected earnings for its most recent quarter, but slightly soft sales weighed on the stock. The company attributed the sales weakness to supply issues in Indonesia and Turkey due to regulatory changes. However, its noncombustible products continue to lead growth. We remain constructive on this cash flow-generative business and are pleased with Philip Morris’s commitment to returning cash to shareholders through reliable dividends and stock repurchases.

Japan’s Shimano, which manufacturers bicycle parts, fishing components, and rowing equipment, lowered its forward guidance during the quarter because of weakness in overseas markets and ongoing inventory adjustments. Our investment thesis remains intact, as we are confident that Shimano can work through accumulated inventories after the strong but unsustainable demand for its products during the Covid-19 era. In addition to high-quality products and dominant global market share, the company has a strong history of returning capital to investors.

Germany based-FUCHS is a global manufacturer and distributor of high-value-add industrial lubricants for a range of applications. The company reported weaker-than-expected results for its most recent quarter due to the impact of tariffs, low industrial output in Europe and geopolitical tensions. We believe these challenges are cyclical in nature, and we continue to like the company’s ability to generate cash flow and grow its business. With concentrated family ownership and a limited free float that restricts buybacks, management has consistently returned capital to shareholders through dividends.

Nufarm (OTCPK:NUFMF) produces agricultural pesticides and develops seed technologies. Continued high leverage, weak prices for Omega-3 canola oil and the potential outcome of a strategic review of Nufarm’s seed technologies business triggered a negative ratings watch from Fitch during the quarter. We continue to anticipate resilient cash flows from Nufarm’s diversified portfolio of crop-protection products and its broad geographic reach.

MileagePlus Holdings owns the assets of United Airlines’ loyalty program and generates revenue by selling miles to partners including banks, credit card companies and United Airlines itself. The bonds referenced above were prepaid during the quarter. Although prepayments reflect the issuer’s financial strength, they tend to drive the market price of an impacted bonds toward the call price.

AMN Healthcare (AMN) provides permanent and temporary staffing—nurses, physicians and allied health professionals—for a wide range of environments including hospitals, clinics, dialysis centers and home health agencies, Performance was pressured during the quarter by operating results, forward guidance and a non-cash charge. We acquired these new-issue bonds—with a higher rate and longer term than the debt they replace—in September, and we believe they enhance AMN’s ability to support its long-term growth objectives.

We appreciate your confidence and thank you for your support. Sincerely,

First Eagle Investments
